OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 67-year-old woman with a longstanding history of rheumatoid factor-positive rheumatoid arthritis (RA) was being treated with subcutaneous methotrexate 10 mg. She went to the emergency department due to sudden dyspnoea and left chest pain.
 Imaging Findings: Chest X-ray showed a moderate left pneumothorax and a cavitated lesion in the subpleural region of the left upper lobe (LUL) (Figure 1a), which was present on a prior chest X-ray (Figure 1b). After a chest tube was placed (Figure 1c), the pneumothorax did not fully resolve, and symptoms persisted, prompting a CT scan.
 
 CT reported a left pneumothorax. The tube was correctly positioned. A cavitated nodule located in the LUL with communication to the pleural cavity was shown in axial (Figure 2a) and coronal planes (Figure 2b). Other broad-based nodules in the pleural surface were identified (Figure 2c). The rest of the parenchyma presented a slight thickening of the interlobular septa with a diffuse bilateral distribution, probably related to RA-associated interstitial lung disease.
 
 A subsequent revaluation, after reviewing the patient’s radiological history, concluded that the image of a cavitated nodule (Figures 2a and 2b) corresponded to the lesion visible in the previous X-ray (Figure 1b), and proposed the differential diagnosis between cavitated lung neoplasia, rheumatoid nodule and other cavitary lesions such as tuberculosis.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 40-year-old woman presented to the gastroenterologist with postprandial fullness and nausea persisting for one year, along with a 4 kg weight loss. She had no abdominal pain and reported normal faeces. Blood tests were normal.
 Imaging Findings: On ultrasound (Figure 1), a suspicion of a thickened stomach wall was raised.
 
 CT (Figures 2a and 2b) confirmed diffuse wall thickening and hyperaemic inner lining of the stomach with a small lumen. Lymphadenopathies were identified in the gastrohepatic and gastrocolic ligaments. No peritoneal metastases were observed on CT, but the lack of significant intra-abdominal fat limited definitive exclusion.
 
 During gastroscopy Figure 3), insufflation of the stomach was difficult, but no focal tumoral process was noted.
 
 Initial MRI (Figures 4a, 4b, and 4c) showed diffusion restriction of the stomach lining. Additionally, multiple subtle peritoneal, mesenteric, and serosal lesions were identified, which were confirmed via diagnostic laparoscopy. Follow-up MRI (Figures 5a and 5b) showed disease progression with increased wall thickening of the stomach in the antrum, pylorus, and duodenal bulb, as well as the development of bilateral ovarian lesions.

- Cons: lymphoma rarely causes bilateral ovarian involvement as primary disease; ovarian lesions less expected in lymphoma (though secondary involvement possible).

- Pros: diffuse wall thickening and hyperemic lining could be inflammatory gastritis; diffusion restriction seen in inflammation.  
- Cons: progressive worsening and development of ovarian lesions unlikely purely inflammatory; lymphadenopathy and peritoneal lesions suggest malignancy; patient's systemic symptoms and weight loss suggest neoplastic process.

- Pros: diffuse gastric wall thickening with hypertrophic inner lining; small lumen due to rugal hypertrophy; protein loss and weight loss common.  
- Cons: no mention of protein loss or hypoalbuminemia; hyperemia and diffusion restriction may not be typical; development of lymphadenopathy and peritoneal/ovarian lesions not consistent with this benign condition.

- Cons: no focal tumor on gastroscopy but linitis plastica tends to show diffuse infiltration rather than discrete mass.

- Pros: peritoneal, mesenteric, and serosal lesions could be metastases; bilateral ovarian lesions suggest Krukenberg tumors (metastatic gastric cancer).  
- Cons: no focal tumor on endoscopy or imaging; diffuse wall thickening is less typical for metastases; initial suspicion of thickened stomach wall could be primary.  

2) Lymphoma  
- Pros: diffuse gastric wall thickening and hyperemia can be lymphoma; bulky lymphadenopathy; diffusion restriction on MRI consistent with lymphoma.  
- Cons: lymphoma rarely causes bilateral ovarian involvement as primary disease; ovarian lesions less expected in lymphoma (though secondary involvement possible).  

3) Inflammation  
- Pros: diffuse wall thickening and hyperemic lining could be inflammatory gastritis; diffusion restriction seen in inflammation.  
- Cons: progressive worsening and development of ovarian lesions unlikely purely inflammatory; lymphadenopathy and peritoneal lesions suggest malignancy; patient's systemic symptoms and weight loss suggest neoplastic process.  

4) Ménétrier disease  
- Pros: diffuse gastric wall thickening with hypertrophic inner lining; small lumen due to rugal hypertrophy; protein loss and weight loss common.  
- Cons: no mention of protein loss or hypoalbuminemia; hyperemia and diffusion restriction may not be typical; development of lymphadenopathy and peritoneal/ovarian lesions not consistent with this benign condition.  

5) Linitis plastica in diffuse stomach carcinoma  
- Pros: classic diffuse wall thickening with small lumen; hyperemic inner lining; difficulty insufflating stomach; peritoneal and serosal spread; bilateral ovarian involvement typical for advanced diffuse gastric carcinoma (Krukenberg tumors).  
- Cons: no focal tumor on gastroscopy but linitis plastica tends to show diffuse infiltration rather than discrete mass.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Linitis plastica in diffuse stomach carcinoma (strongly supported by diffuse thickening, hyperemia, peritoneal involvement, bilateral ovarian lesions, difficulty insufflation).  
2) Metastases (supported by peritoneal and ovarian lesions but less by focal tumor).  
3) Lymphoma (supported by diffusion restriction and adenopathy but less by ovarian lesions).  
4) Ménétrier disease (diffuse hypertrophy fits, but no protein loss/inflammation and progressive malignant features less consistent).  
5) Inflammation (least likely given systemic features and progression).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 62-year-old male patient presented to the emergency room with visual disturbances and right-hand weakness. He had a medical history of alcoholism, tobacco use, hyperlipidaemia, and traumatic splenectomy, and was hospitalised due to an ischemic stroke. He underwent routine diagnostics, where, from laboratory tests, only hyperlipidaemia was clinically significant.
 Imaging Findings: Routine abdominal ultrasound revealed an asymptomatic, well-defined, homogeneous, hypoechogenic mass measuring 46 x 36 mm in the 8th liver segment (Figure 1).
 
 On abdominal multidetector computerised tomography (MDCT), the mass was ill-defined and isodense to the surrounding liver parenchyma on unenhanced scans (Figure 2a). Following intravenous contrast, the lesion demonstrated increased attenuation (Figure 2b), whereas, in the latter phases, it was isodense with the surrounding liver parenchyma (Figure 2c). Other findings included two splenunculi, one near the lesser curvature of the stomach (Figure 2d) and one in the left subphrenic region (Figure 2e).
 
 Contrast abdominal MR scans confirmed a well-defined, slightly T2-hyperintense mass measuring 43 x 30 mm (Figure 3a). The mass showed poor uniform post-contrast enhancement in the arterial phase (Figure 3b), following washout in later phases (Figure 3c). The mass showed significant restricted diffusion on DWI (Figure 3d) and low ADC values (Figure 3e).

OriginalDescription:

 Diffusion restriction on DWI with low ADC values (Figures 1d, 1e, 2d, 2e, 3d, 3e, 4d, and 4e).
 
 Additional involvement of the bilateral fronto-parieto-temporal cortex and corticospinal tracts involving the posterior limb of the internal capsule and midbrain.
 
 No post-contrast enhancement (Figures 6a and 6b).

2) Wernickeâs encephalopathy â Alcoholism fits but corpus callosum not typical; other classic sites absent.

4) Toxic-metabolic encephalopathy â Vague; imaging does not favor.  
5) Hypoxic-ischemic encephalopathy â No clinical hypoxia indicated; imaging less supportive.  

Hence, the most likely diagnosis is Acute Marchiafava-Bignami disease.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 64-year-old man with a history of smoking and occasional alcohol use presented with a foreign body sensation and odynophagia. Clinical examination showed a bulge on the right lateral pharyngeal wall with normal mucosa and palatine tonsil. Neurological exam and lab tests, particularly white blood cell count, SR and RCP, were unremarkable.
 Imaging Findings: The patient underwent an MR scan showing a 20 x 35 x 43.5 mm cystic mass in the right retropharyngeal space (RPS) (Figures 1a and 1b), bulging the prevertebral muscles and impinging on the oropharynx, with leftward deviation and reduced airway patency. The mass was spontaneously hyperintense to muscle on T1W images (Figure 2), hyperintense on T2W images, with a thin septation on T2 FS images (Figure 3). There was no restricted diffusion on the ADC map (Figure 4), and no enhancement was noted on subtracted T1W images before and after gadolinium administration (Figures 5a and 5b). There was no oedema in the surrounding tissues, and no abnormalities were seen in the pharynx, larynx, oesophagus, thyroid, or cervical lymph nodes.
 
 A comparison with an MR scan from 20 years ago showed the lesion was already present but smaller (10 x 20 x 35 mm) (Figure 6). At that time, the MRI was performed for unrelated reasons, and the incidental retropharyngeal lesion was interpreted as a presumed vascular malformation. Since the patient was asymptomatic, follow-up observation and monitoring was implemented, but the patient was lost to follow-up after a few years.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 75-year-old male patient with a history of non-metastatic urothelial carcinoma and prostate cancer, both successfully treated surgically two years ago. During follow-up, a palpable, hard, and fixed cervical mass was detected. No history of neck trauma or radiation.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound showed a 6 cm solid and heterogeneous left mass with internal calcifications and intralesional vascularisation, not arising from the thyroid gland (Figures 1a and 1b). This mass was displacing and compressing the left internal jugular vein, which was still permeable (Figures 1c, 1d, and 1e). Findings were consistent with a calcified adenopathy.
 
 A subsequent non-contrast CT due to renal impairment revealed a solid left supraclavicular mass, with a heterogeneous density, irregular outlines, and thick calcifications. Thickening of the lateral-cervical ipsilateral musculature was also observed (Figures 2a and 2b). There was a pathological adenopathy with calcifications at the Va cervical level (Figure 2c).
 
 A core needle biopsy was performed, and histology reported infiltration of spindle-shaped, pleomorphic, and atypical cells, followed by the formation of an osteoid matrix and chondroid tissue (Figures 3a and 3b).

   - Pros: Calcifications and ossification in soft tissue.
   - Cons: Typically shows zoning phenomenon (peripheral mature bone ring with central immature tissue); usually associated with trauma; no history of trauma; mass is large, irregular, and malignant-like; histology with malignant spindle cells and osteoid matrix contradicts benign process.

   - Pros: Calcified lymph nodes possible in these conditions.
   - Cons: Clinical context lacks infection or systemic inflammatory signs; histology shows malignant spindle cells and matrix production; mass is large, aggressive; no granulomas or typical benign features.

   - Pros: Malignant spindle cells present; aggressive imaging features.
   - Cons: Histology specifically shows osteoid and chondroid matrix production, consistent with osteosarcoma rather than other sarcomas.

2) Another type of high-grade sarcoma - possible but lacks specific osteoid/chondroid matrix typically described in osteosarcoma.
3) Calcified metastatic lymphadenopathy - less likely due to sarcomatous histology.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A sixteen-year-old male patient presented with congenital deafness on the left side and normal hearing on the right side. There was a family history of deafness and hearing loss on the father’s side. He experienced relatively few difficulties in daily life. Audiometry showed near-total deafness on the left side and normal thresholds on the right.
 Imaging Findings: A 3T MR study of the temporal bones was performed. The thin, heavily T2-weighted images revealed a bud-like, cystic cochlea on the left side with absent normal turns, modiolus and interscalar septa (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c and 6). Additionally, there was no identifiable left cochlear nerve, and apart from the facial nerve, there was only one other nerve, smaller in calibre compared to the facial nerve, in the internal auditory canal, consistent with a hypoplastic vestibulocochlear nerve and cochlear nerve aplasia (Figures 3a, 4a, 4b, 4c, 4d, 4e, and 4f). The vestibular nerve did not bifurcate into a superior and inferior branch. The left inner ear canal was small in calibre as well, progressively narrowing from medial to lateral. The left vestibule appeared mildly globose (Figure 2). The left lateral semicircular canal exhibited focal signal loss, which could either be a true abnormality or an artefact. The inner ear structures and inner ear canal on the right side were normally developed (Figures 1a, 1b, 3b, 5a, 5b, 5c, 5d, 5e, and 5f).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 29-year-old male with a history of recurrent urolithiasis presented with persistent abdominal pain and unexplained fever. Blood tests revealed elevated inflammatory markers and impaired renal function.
 Imaging Findings: On the CT scan, the left kidney is markedly enlarged, and multiple kidney calculi, including a staghorn calculus, are identified within the calyces and renal pelvis (Figures 1a and 2a). Diffuse thinning of the renal cortex and non-homogeneous, poorly differentiated renal parenchyma, replaced by multiple hypodense fluid-filled cavities (mean attenuation ~ +11 HU) (Figure 1b), with rim enhancement (Figure 3), consistent with the “bear paw sign”, associated with minimal air-fluid levels. Fat-density areas (mean attenuation ~ -60 HU) within and adjacent to the fluid collections (xanthomas) are seen (Figure 1c). The renal pelvis and calyces are dilated. The perirenal fat stranding and thickening of both Gerota’s and Zuckerkandl’s fasciae are noted, indicating stage II–III inflammatory extension. The entire ureter shows diffuse wall thickening with fat stranding and linear calcifications in the mid-ureter (Figure 2b). On the delayed urographic phase, there is no contrast excretion, confirming impaired left kidney function (Figure 4a and 4b).

A) Key findings:

- Diffuse thinning of renal cortex; renal parenchyma replaced by multiple hypodense fluid-filled cavities (~+11 HU) with rim enhancement, creating a "bear paw sign".
- Fat-density areas (~-60 HU) within and adjacent to fluid collections (xanthomas).
- Dilated renal pelvis and calyces.
- Perirenal fat stranding and thickening of Gerota’s and Zuckerkandl’s fasciae (Stage II-III inflammatory extension).
- Diffuse ureteral wall thickening and linear calcifications.

- Pros: Can cause renal enlargement and heterogeneous parenchyma.
- Cons: Typically does not present with multiple fluid-filled cystic lesions with rim enhancement (bear paw sign); fat-density areas (xanthomas) and staghorn calculi are not characteristic; no delayed contrast excretion expected.

2) Abscess:
- Pros: Rim-enhancing fluid collections and fever support infection.
- Cons: Renal abscess usually presents as localized collection rather than diffuse parenchymal replacement; does not typically include multiple calculi or xanthomas; "bear paw sign" is not typical.

3) Renal tuberculosis:
- Pros: Can cause kidney enlargement and cystic changes.
- Cons: Usually presents with multiple strictures and calcifications but not staghorn calculi; xanthomas uncommon; "bear paw sign" not typical; clinical history and imaging more consistent with another diagnosis.

4) Xanthogranulomatous pyelonephritis (XGP):
- Pros: Classic "bear paw sign" (multiple hypodense fluid-filled cavities with rim enhancement); presence of staghorn calculi; fat-density xanthomas within affected kidney; enlarged kidney; perirenal fat stranding and thickening of Gerota's fascia indicate inflammatory extension; impaired renal function and clinical infection signs fit well.
- Cons: None significant.

5) Lymphoma:
- Pros: Can enlarge kidney.
- Cons: Does not involve stones or "bear paw sign"; no calcifications; no signs of infection; no xanthomas; clinical presentation inconsistent.

6) Malakoplakia:
- Pros: Can cause renal inflammation and enlargement.
- Cons: Rare; does not typically cause staghorn calculi or "bear paw sign"; no fat-density xanthomas; clinical and imaging features less typical.

1) Xanthogranulomatous pyelonephritis (definitive imaging features including bear paw sign, staghorn calculus, xanthomas, and inflammatory extension match perfectly).
2) Renal carcinoma (less likely due to infection signs and fat-density xanthomas).
3) Abscess (infection present but imaging less consistent).
4) Renal tuberculosis.
5) Malakoplakia.
6) Lymphoma.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 29-year-old female patient presented to the cardiology department with episodes of numbness in both her upper and lower extremities, which spontaneously resolved after 30 minutes. She also reported episodes of chest pain that she attributed to stress. Heart auscultation was normal. ECG showed diffuse ST-elevations.
 Imaging Findings: Echocardiography (ECG) demonstrated severe systolic heart failure, with a left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF) of 30%. The exam showed basal hypokinesia and preserved apical contractility, along with irregularities in the lining of the left ventricular myocardium (Figure 1).
 
 A contrast-enhanced computed tomography (CECT) scan revealed no coronary abnormalities but confirmed the irregularities in the left ventricle wall (Figure 2).
 
 Subsequently, a cardiac MRI was performed, which revealed moderate left ventricle dilation and irregularities in the ventricle wall, including protrusions of myocardial bridges, multiple crypts, and thinning of the inferolateral apex with diffuse dyskinesia (Figures 3 and 4). Severe left ventricular dysfunction was confirmed, with an LVEF of 20% and a global longitudinal strain (GLS) of -7.85%, as well as ventricular dyssynchrony. A non-ischemic fibrosis region was also present in the interventricular septum and the posterior hinge point. The right ventricle appeared to be normal in morphology and function.
PostDescription:

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 29-year-old female with transient limb numbness and chest pain attributed to stress, normal heart auscultation.  
- ECG showing diffuse ST-elevations.  
- Severe systolic heart failure with LVEF 20%-30% and global longitudinal strain severely reduced (-7.85%).  
- Basal hypokinesia with apical sparing (i.e., preserved apical contractility).  
- Left ventricular wall irregularities: protruding myocardial bridges, multiple crypts, thinning of inferolateral apex, diffuse dyskinesia.  
- Non-ischemic fibrosis in interventricular septum and posterior hinge point.  
- No coronary artery abnormalities on CECT.  
- Normal right ventricle.  
- Ventricular dyssynchrony noted.  

B) Candidate pass/fail with pros/cons:  

1) Left ventricular noncompaction (LVNC):  
Pros: Irregularities in the left ventricular wall, presence of multiple crypts could suggest prominent trabeculations which are characteristic of LVNC. However, no specific mention of classic trabeculated myocardium with a noncompacted to compacted ratio >2.3:1 is given.  
Cons: Basal hypokinesia and apical sparing are atypical; LVNC generally shows prominent trabeculations mainly at the apical and mid-ventricular segments. Also, crypts are not the typical description for LVNC (trabeculations are). Moreover, "crypts" are less commonly described in LVNC. Fibrosis can be present but non-ischemic fibrosis at septal and posterior hinge point is more characteristic of other cardiomyopathies.  

2) Saw-tooth cardiomyopathy:  
Pros: This is a very rare cardiomyopathy characterized by saw-tooth appearance on imaging due to multiple wall irregularities. The described protruding myocardial bridges and multiple crypts can cause an irregular left ventricular cavity border, which is typical of saw-tooth or endocardial fibroelastosis variants.  
Cons: Saw-tooth cardiomyopathy is exceedingly rare with few case reports; typically described in younger patients and sometimes associated with apical akinesia/dyskinesia with fibrosis and wall irregularities. The presence of basal hypokinesia with apical sparing is less typical. However, the irregularities described match well. The presence of a non-ischemic fibrosis in specific locations also fits.  

3) Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM):  
Pros: Presence of non-ischemic fibrosis in septal and hinge point areas, basal hypokinesia, and LV dysfunction can be seen in HCM. Crypts are also a known feature of HCM, especially early disease. Myocardial bridges are common.  
Cons: No mention of hypertrophy or asymmetric septal hypertrophy, which are hallmarks of HCM; LVEF is severely reduced, but HCM usually has normal or hyperdynamic systolic function. The apical sparing of contractility is opposite of typical HCM where apical regions can be involved. Dilated LV and dystrophic changes are less common in classic HCM.  

4) Arrhythmogenic cardiomyopathy (AC):  
Pros: Non-ischemic fibrosis is typical in arrhythmogenic cardiomyopathy involving the left ventricle (especially the lateral and inferolateral walls). Crypts are less typical but wall abnormalities and fibrosis can be focal.  
Cons: Usually involves the right ventricle or biventricular; here right ventricle is normal. LV involvement alone is less common but possible. Arrhythmogenic cardiomyopathy tends to show fatty infiltration not described here, and wall irregularities are more fat and scar based rather than protruding bridges or crypts.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Saw-tooth cardiomyopathy: Best matches the described LV wall irregularities (protruding myocardial bridges, multiple crypts), non-ischemic fibrosis, severe LV dysfunction, and basal hypokinesia with apical sparing. The saw-tooth appearance is described by irregular endocardial border, fitting here.  
2) Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy: Presence of crypts and fibrosis fits, but lack of hypertrophy and severe reduction in LVEF argue against.  
3) Left ventricular noncompaction: Irregularities could suggest but lack of typical trabeculations and non-specific crypts weaken this.  
4) Arrhythmogenic cardiomyopathy: Lack of right ventricular involvement and specific fatty infiltration makes this less likely.  
Overall, saw-tooth cardiomyopathy best fits the imaging description.  
</analysis>  
<final>  
Saw-tooth cardiomyopathy  
</final><|return|>

No evidence of any significant diffusion restriction was seen in this region (Figures 5a and 5b). Surrounding oedematous changes were seen in the left masticator muscles and infratemporal fossa. No focal discrete collection or solid component was seen.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 2-year-old male child was brought by his 34-year-old mother with complaints of abdominal pain and recurrent vomiting. The child was clinically stable, and the abdominal ultrasound was normal. However, we incidentally discovered that both the mother and the son had physical impairments in their bilateral upper and lower limbs.
 Imaging Findings: The child had a classical median cleft with rudimentary bilateral thumbs with loss of opposition movement (Figure 1). A frontal radiograph revealed a bilateral median cleft, and the right hand’s proximal phalanges of the middle and index fingers fused, resulting in syndactyly. An additional middle phalanx was observed in the right thumb. Bilaterally, the thumb and index finger were displaced toward each other (Figures 2a and 2b). The radiography of the child’s feet depicted the absence of three of his metatarsals (Figure 2c). The X-ray of the infant’s skull showed multiple convolutions, suggesting a copper-beaten appearance (Figure 3).
 
 Interestingly, his 34-year-old mother, too, suffered from a similar deformity. On clinical inspection, both her right and left hands showed the presence of only three fingers (Figure 4a). A hypoplastic index finger was seen in the left hand. Bilateral feet showed only two toes with possible syndactyly (Figure 4b).

A) Key findings:  
- Patient is a 2-year-old male child with abdominal symptoms but clinically stable; abdominal US normal.  
- Incidental finding: Bilateral upper and lower limb deformities in child and mother.  
- Hand: Classical median cleft deformity, rudimentary bilateral thumbs with loss of opposition, syndactyly (proximal phalanges of middle and index fingers fused on right hand), additional middle phalanx in right thumb, displaced thumb and index fingers.  
- Feet: Absence of three metatarsals bilaterally.  
- Skull X-ray: Multiple convolutions with copper-beaten appearance.  
- Mother shows similar deformities: Only three fingers on each hand, hypoplastic index finger on left, only two toes bilaterally with suspected syndactyly.  
- Family history suggests a genetic condition affecting limb development, particularly median cleft hand (ectrodactyly) and split foot deformities.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Syndactyla:  
Pros: Syndactyly described (fusion of fingers).  
Cons: Does not explain median cleft, absent metatarsals, or copper-beaten skull. Syndactyly alone is insufficient diagnostic explanation.  

2) Roberts syndrome:  
Pros: Characteristic for severe limb reduction defects including absent bones and deformities; known to cause symmetrical limb abnormalities.  
Cons: Usually severe microcephaly, facial anomalies; median cleft hand is less typical. Not classically associated with copper-beaten skull or family history at mother's age with mild phenotype.  

3) Split hand/split foot malformation (SHFM):  
Pros: Classical median cleft hand (ectrodactyly), presence of only three fingers on each hand, absent metatarsals, syndactyly in feet, matches the description. Family history possible, variable expressivity.  
Cons: None major; hallmark is median cleft hand with missing fingers/metacarpals/metatarsals.  

4) Ectrodactyly-ectodermal dysplasia-cleft lip and palate (EEC):  
Pros: Ectrodactyly present.  
Cons: No mention of ectodermal dysplasia signs (skin, hair, teeth) or cleft lip/palate in child or mother. Copper-beaten skull not related.  

5) Amniotic band sequence:  
Pros: Can cause limb defects, amputations.  
Cons: Usually unilateral, random, no family history or symmetric pattern, no median cleft deformity, and absent metatarsals is less typical.  

6) Lacrimo-Auriculo-dento-digital syndrome (LADD):  
Pros: Limb anomalies possible.  
Cons: Main features are lacrimal and salivary gland hypoplasia, ear, and dental anomalies, not median cleft hand or absent metatarsals. Unlikely.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Split hand/split foot malformation (SHFM): Fits median cleft hand (ectrodactyly), split foot (absent metatarsals), syndactyly, familial occurrence, and variable expressivity.  
2) Roberts syndrome: Severe limb defects, but lacks hallmark facial and cranial features and severity.  
3) EEC: Lack of ectodermal and orofacial features.  
4) Amniotic band: Unlikely familial and bilateral symmetric deformities.  
5) Lacrimo-Auriculo-dento-digital syndrome (LADD): Doesn't match limb defects.  
6) Syndactyla: Not diagnostic alone.  

Thus, the best fitting diagnosis is Split hand/split foot malformation (SHFM).  
</analysis>  
<final>Split hand/split foot malformation (SHFM)</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 33-year-old female presents with a 6-year history of abnormal involuntary movements of all limbs, face, and lips, which initially started as increased fidgeting and finger flicking. Cognitive decline began a year later, with memory loss, disorientation, and difficulty dressing and eating, and now is fully dependent for activities of daily living.
 Imaging Findings: A contrast-enhanced MRI (CEMRI) of the brain was performed with 1.5T with sequences of T1WI inversion recovery 3D, T2WI axial and coronal, 3D FLAIR, SWI, DWI, and MR spectroscopy (MRS). MRI revealed atrophy of the head of the caudate bilaterally (Figures 1 and 2) with decreased FH:CC ratio of 1.18 (frontal horn width to intercaudate distance ratio) (Figure 5) and increased CC:IT ratio of 0.25 (intercaudate distance to inner table width ratio) (Figure 6). Volume loss of the putamen and globus pallidus was noted bilaterally. An enlargement of the bilateral frontal horns of the lateral ventricles, giving a box-like configuration, is noted. There is evidence of T2/FLAIR hyperintensities (Figures 3 and 4) seen in the involved region with mild grade II mineralisation seen in globi pallidi, extending to bilateral posterolateral putamen and also in the substantia nigra. Mild prominence of sulci and ventricles with grey matter volume loss is suggestive of mild cerebral volume loss. MRS shows choline peak and mild NAA (N-acetyl aspartate) loss in the basal ganglia (Figures 7a, 7b, 7c, and 7d).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: 8-year-old female with swelling in the dorsal aspect of the tongue on the left side, present since birth, with slight enlargement in the past 6 months. No history of fever, breathing difficulties, or bleeding tendency. On physical examination, it showed a bluish swelling in the anterior tongue measuring 1.5 x 1 cm, and it was a soft, non-tender mass on palpation (Figure 1).
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound of the dorsal aspect of the tongue showed a well-circumscribed hypoechoic lesion with smooth margins in the anterior aspect of the tongue on the left side. No evidence of calcifications (phleboliths) or cystic areas. No surrounding induration was seen. Colour Doppler studies showed mild internal vascularity (Figure 2).
 
 MRI showed a well-defined T2 intensely hyperintense (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c), and iso to slightly hyperintense signal in T1-weighted images (Figure 5) in the anterior aspect of the tongue on the left side. No blooming artefacts on the gradient echo sequences (GRE) images (Figure 4). No evidence of true diffusion restriction. Homogeneous contrast enhancement on post-contrast T1 images was seen (Figure 6). Delayed post-contrast images are not available due to patient motion artefacts.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 6-year-old child with a diagnosis of Gorlin syndrome, so far manifested by medulloblastoma resected two years ago, and disease-free in subsequent follow-ups until now, presented with insidious hip pain. Given her medical history, a pelvic CT was performed.
 Imaging Findings: Brain MRI, performed 2 years previously, at the time of medulloblastoma diagnosis, revealed a mottled intraparenchymal mass in the posterior fossa with heterogeneous enhancement and hydrocephalus (Figures 1a and 1b).
 
 Actual pelvic CT showed extensive osteolytic involvement with a soft tissue component affecting all included bony structures (vertebral bodies, pelvic girdle, and femurs), with severe destruction of the pelvis and both femoral heads (Figures 2a and 2b). Given the presence of lytic metastases, a subsequent whole-body CT with intravenous contrast in the portal phase was performed, revealing retroperitoneal adenopathic conglomerates (Figure 3) and a single hepatic metastasis (Figure 4).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 56-year-old woman presented with a painless right breast lump, present for three months. She also reported easy fatigability, ptosis, and exertional dyspnoea for one year. She was diagnosed with myasthenia gravis, and was started on immunosuppressants five months ago. There was no history of tuberculosis (TB) or contact with TB patients.
 Imaging Findings: High-resolution CT of the thorax showed a well-defined soft tissue density in the anterior superior mediastinum. CT-guided biopsy revealed polygonal epithelial cells with interspersed lymphocytes, suggestive of thymoma type B1. Only non-contrast-enhanced CT of the thorax was performed (Figures 1a, 1b, and 1c) due to contraindication to iodinated contrast in myasthenia gravis. An elliptical pleural-based hyperdense lesion with peripheral calcification and central fluid density was noted along the lateral right hemithorax, suggestive of empyema. A lobulated soft tissue lesion was seen in the lower inner quadrant of the right breast, extending into the underlying pectoral muscle. Ultrasound correlation was advised.
 
 Ultrasound of the right breast (Figures 2a, 2b, and 3) revealed an irregular, heterogeneously hypoechoic lesion with posterior enhancement, oriented parallel to the parenchyma from 3 to 5 o’clock position, 2 cm from the nipple–areola complex. No internal vascularity or calcification was seen. The lesion extended into the pectoralis muscle and appeared soft on elastography. It was categorised as BIRADS 4A.
 
 Fine-needle aspiration cytology was advised (Figures 4a and 4b).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 18-year-old female who recently migrated with her family due to war presented with longstanding, progressive motor impairment of unclear origin. She was referred by an outpatient neurologist after multiple sclerosis had been excluded. No previous medical records were available from her country of origin.
 
 According to her mother, she developed normally in her early infancy, walking independently. Around 17–18 months, shortly after vaccination (possibly MMR), she developed dragging of one leg, recurrent fevers, and loss of motor milestones. A verbal diagnosis of post-viral encephalitis was given at the time. Her condition fluctuated, but cognition remained intact. She developed progressive gait decline during her childhood. Wheelchair use began at age 15, and she is now fully dependent for transfers, though she eats and writes independently.
 
 Clinical and neurological examination revealed esophoria, mild tongue deviation, weak facial muscles, hypotrophic upper extremities with reduced fine motor control, joint hypermobility, increased tone and reflexes with bilateral positive Trömner signs, and severe leg-dominant spastic tetraparesis.
 
 Given the unclear clinical presentation, brain and spinal MRI were performed.
 Imaging Findings: Brain MRI revealed symmetrical T2 (Figures 1) and FLAIR (Figures 2) hyperintensities in the supratentorial white matter (excluding U-fibres), splenium of the corpus callosum, posterior limb of the internal capsule, pons along trigeminal pathways, inferior cerebellar peduncles, and pyramidal tracts of the medulla oblongata. DWI (Figures 3) showed restricted diffusion with low ADC (Figures 4). No contrast enhancement was observed (Figures 5). MR spectroscopy (Figures 6) revealed a lactate peak at the level of the abnormal white matter. Spinal MRI (Figures 7 and 8) displayed longitudinal signal abnormalities predominantly involving the dorsal columns and lateral corticospinal tracts throughout the entire cord. Findings suggested a metabolic leukodystrophy. Diagnosis was confirmed by genetic testing.

model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>
A) Key findings:
- 18-year-old female with progressive motor impairment beginning after vaccination at ~17-18 months.
- Clinical: severe spastic tetraparesis with upper motor neuron signs, hypotrophic limbs, increased tone, hyperreflexia.
- MRI brain: symmetrical T2/FLAIR hyperintensity in supratentorial white matter (excluding U-fibers), splenium of corpus callosum, posterior limb of internal capsule, pons along trigeminal pathways, inferior cerebellar peduncles, pyramidal tracts of medulla.
- DWI: restricted diffusion with low ADC in abnormal white matter.
- MR spectroscopy: lactate peak in affected white matter.
- Spinal MRI: longitudinal signal abnormalities involving dorsal columns and corticospinal tracts throughout the cord.
- No contrast enhancement.
- Diagnosis confirmed by genetic testing.
- Suggestive of metabolic leukodystrophy with characteristic pattern: brainstem, spinal cord involvement, lactate peak.

B) Candidate pass/fail:

1) Hypomyelination with brainstem and spinal cord involvement (HBSL)
   - Pros: Involvement of brainstem and spinal cord white matter. Age can be pediatric.
   - Cons: HBSL typically does not show restricted diffusion or lactate peak. U-fibers often involved. Genetic testing usually for DARS2 gene. Clinical onset often younger, and clinical course more stable.

2) Leukoencephalopathy with brainstem and spinal cord involvement and lactate elevation (LBSL)
   - Pros: Classic involvement of posterior limb of internal capsule, cerebral peduncles, inferior cerebellar peduncles, dorsal columns and corticospinal tracts of spinal cord. Lactate peak is characteristic. Onset in infancy/early childhood with progressive motor decline. DWI restricted diffusion is reported. Genetic confirmation (DARS2 mutation) is standard.
   - Cons: None significant, fits well.

3) Krabbe disease
   - Pros: Presents with motor impairment, spastic and dystonic symptoms. MRI shows extensive white matter involvement.
   - Cons: Typically involves deep cerebellar white matter, thalami, and corticospinal tracts, not sparing U-fibers. Usually more diffuse and progressive demyelination. No lactate peak. Clinical onset may be earlier or later. Spinal MRI less characteristically involved in this pattern.

4) X-linked adrenoleukodystrophy
   - Pros: Young onset, white matter abnormalities.
   - Cons: Usually involves parieto-occipital white matter asymmetrically with contrast enhancement. No consistent spinal cord involvement at this pattern. No lactate peak. Female less common and clinical picture differs.

5) Metachromatic leukodystrophy
   - Pros: Progressive motor and cognitive decline, white matter involvement.
   - Cons: Typically involves periventricular white matter, U-fibers are often involved. No lactate peak. Spinal cord involvement possible but pattern differs. Age of onset early childhood, but MRI pattern less specific to brainstem and spinal cord as in this case.

6) Alexander disease
   - Pros: Leukodystrophy with frontal predominance.
   - Cons: Typically frontal periventricular white matter involvement, astrocytic inclusions. No lactate peak. Spinal cord involvement unusual. Clinical presentation different.

C) Ranking:
1) Leukoencephalopathy with brainstem and spinal cord involvement and lactate elevation (LBSL) — matches imaging distribution (posterior limb of internal capsule, pons, inferior cerebellar peduncles, dorsal columns, corticospinal tracts), lactate peak, clinical course, and genetic confirmation.
2) Hypomyelination with brainstem and spinal cord involvement (HBSL) — similar areas but lack of lactate peak and absence of U-fiber hyperintensity argue against.
3) Metachromatic leukodystrophy
4) Krabbe disease
5) Alexander disease
6) X-linked adrenoleukodystrophy

<final>Leukoencephalopathy with brainstem and spinal cord involvement and lactate elevation (LBSL)</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 21-year-old Caucasian man, an amateur drummer, presents with a two-year history of progressive weakness of the left distal hand. There is no associated pain or sensory loss. Past medical and family history are unremarkable. There is no history of preceding trauma.
 Imaging Findings: On MRI performed with the neck in the neutral position, T2 and T1-weighted sagittal images (Figures 1 and 2, respectively) reveal mild spinal cord atrophy at the C5–C7 level. On T2 axial images (Figure 3), atrophy of the left anterior horn is clearly visible.
 
 In the flexed position (Figure 4), there is an anterior shift of the posterior wall of the cervical dural sac at the C3–Th3 level. A crescent-shaped epidural venous plexus appears in the posterior epidural space, showing high signal intensity on both T1 and T2-weighted sequences and is accompanied by flow voids.

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 21-year-old man with progressive unilateral distal hand weakness over two years.  
- MRI in neutral position: mild atrophy of the cervical spinal cord at C5-C7 level.  
- T2 axial images: left anterior horn cell atrophy.  
- MRI in flexed position: anterior shift of the posterior wall of the cervical dural sac at C3–Th3 level.  
- Crescent-shaped epidural venous plexus in posterior epidural space with high signal on T1 and T2, with flow voids.  

B) Candidate pass/fail with pros/cons:  
1) Young-onset amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS):  
   Pros: Progressive muscle weakness, young age.  
   Cons: ALS usually bilateral and diffuse, no cervical cord flexion abnormalities; MRI findings are not typical for ALS; no frontotemporal degeneration clinically or radiologically described. Anterior horn cell atrophy is seen but the flexion-dependent dural shift and epidural venous plexus are not characteristic of ALS.  

2) Spinal muscular atrophy (SMA):  
   Pros: Anterior horn cell involvement, progressive weakness.  
   Cons: SMA is usually infantile or early childhood onset; no mention of flexion-dependent cord changes or epidural venous plexus. No family history or genetics supporting SMA.  

3) Anterior horn syndrome:  
   Pros: Anterior horn cell atrophy present; unilateral weakness fits.  
   Cons: Anterior horn syndrome is a clinical syndrome with varied causes, but the MRI flexion-dependent dural shift and epidous venous plexus are specific for Hirayama disease, not generic anterior horn syndrome.  

4) Hirayama disease:  
   Pros: Classic age and sex (young male), unilateral distal hand weakness, insidious onset over years; MRI findings classic: atrophy of anterior horn cells, flexion-dependent anterior displacement of posterior dura, crescent-shaped posterior epidural venous plexus with high T1/T2 signal (indicating congestion/slow flow).  
   Cons: None significant.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Hirayama disease - matches clinical and pathognomonic flexion MRI findings and progression pattern.  
2) Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis - possible but lacks typical bilateral or widespread involvement and MRI findings do not support it here.  
3) Anterior horn syndrome - more generic; lacks MRI specifics.  
4) Spinal muscular atrophy - less likely given age and MRI flexion changes.

<final>Hirayama disease</final><|return|>
options: Young-onset amyotrophic lateral sclerosis | Spinal muscular atrophy | Anterior horn syndrome | Hirayama disease

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 81-year-old man was referred with a four-month history of persistent right upper quadrant pain radiating to the back. Liver function tests were normal, and he denied fever or pruritus. His medical history included pacemaker implantation, malaria, appendicectomy, arterial hypertension, and orthopaedic surgeries. No alcohol abuse or smoking was reported.
 Imaging Findings: The patient first underwent abdominal ultrasound and contrast-enhanced CT scan (Figures 1a and 1b), which revealed consistent findings: gallbladder hydrops with thin walls, moderate dilatation of the intrahepatic right bile ducts, and an ill-defined 1.5 cm soft-tissue lesion at the junction of the cystic duct and the common bile duct.
 
 Gadoxetic acid-enhanced MRI (Figures 2a and 2b) with magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography (MRCP) (Figures 3a and 3b) confirmed tissue infiltration at the junction of the right anterior segmental duct and common bile duct, explaining the dilatation of the right anterior bile ducts.
 
 Endoscopic ultrasound and endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography (ERCP) (Figure 4) were subsequently performed to perform biopsies and to place a plastic biliary stent.

Clinical History: Case 1
 
 A 28-year-old patient with known homozygous sickle cell disease (HbSS) presented with chronic fatigue, diffuse abdominal pain, and hip joint discomfort exacerbated by walking.
 
 Case 2
 
 A 34-year-old patient with a known history of sickle cell disease underwent CT for left upper quadrant abdominal pain and chronic fatigue.
 Imaging Findings: Case 1
 
 Abdominal CT scan (Figures 1a and 1b) revealed a markedly reduced spleen size, with the parenchyma completely replaced by dense, coarse calcifications.  Bone window CT images through the pelvis revealed bilateral femoral head avascular necrosis (AVN), with subchondral collapse and cortical irregularities (Figures 2a and 2b).
 
 Case 2
 
 Abdominal CT scan (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, and 3d) demonstrated a severely atrophied spleen appearing as a densely calcified structure in the left upper quadrant. The calcifications are confluent and coarse, with no visible soft tissue component. Post-contrast images showed no contrast enhancement, and no other abdominal or bony abnormalities were identified.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 20-year-old man with a history of mixed non-seminomatous germ cell tumour with focal invasion of the vascular tunic (pT1N2M0, S2 – Stage IIIB) underwent radical orchiectomy, adjuvant chemotherapy, and retroperitoneal lymphadenectomy, remaining under surveillance with serum markers and body CT scans. An axillary swelling was noticed on physical examination, and a chest CT scan was ordered.
 Imaging Findings: In the left axillary region, an oblong structure measuring approximately 60 mm in longitudinal extension is identified, isodense to the muscular planes. This image extends from the lateral border of the latissimus dorsi muscle to the pectoralis major muscle.
PostDescription:

- New chest CT ordered due to axillary swelling.
- Imaging: left axilla, oblong structure, ~60 mm longitudinally, isodense to muscle, extending from lateral border of latissimus dorsi to pectoralis major.

B) Candidate pass/fail:

1) Adenopathy (reactive or malignant)
   - Pros: Axillary swelling in a cancer patient; length ~60 mm consistent with lymph node; location in axilla fits typical node stations.
   - Cons: Usually lymph nodes are more nodular or rounded than oblong; description as "oblong structure isodense to muscle" less typical; no mention of central necrosis or cluster; however, treated patient with N2 history makes adenopathy plausible.
   
2) Desmoid tumour
   - Pros: Desmoid tumours can arise in muscle planes and fascial layers; isodense to muscle is consistent; location between latissimus dorsi and pectoralis major is plausible; size fits.
   - Cons: Desmoids are usually not common in this age group without underlying risk factor; no mention of infiltrative margins or spiculated appearance; less likely as first diagnosis in a cancer patient.

3) Epidermal inclusion cysts
   - Pros: Common in subcutaneous tissues; isodense to soft tissue.
   - Cons: Usually small, round, superficial, often with central puncta; rarely 6 cm and deep between major muscles; location deep in axilla less typical.

4) Langer’s axillary arch
   - Pros: Defined anatomic variant - additional muscular slip connecting latissimus dorsi to pectoralis major or coracobrachialis; would appear as an oblong muscle-like structure extending between these muscles; isodense to muscle.
   - Cons: Usually congenital, not acquired; can mimic pathology, but size of 60 mm is plausible for a muscular band; can be asymptomatic or palpable.

5) Accessory breast tissue
   - Pros: Can be located in axilla.
   - Cons: Usually soft tissue density, not isodense to muscle; size variable but often small; no description of fat content or typical breast tissue characteristics; location more superficial.

C) Ranking:
1) Langer’s axillary arch - best fits description: oblong muscle isodense structure extending from latissimus dorsi to pectoralis major; typical location and appearance.
2) Adenopathy - plausible but less consistent morphology and description; would expect nodal shape rather than muscle-like oblong.
3) Desmoid tumour - possible but no imaging details suggest infiltrative mass; size large but appearance less typical.
4) Accessory breast tissue - less likely due to muscle density and location.
5) Epidermal inclusion cyst - least likely due to size, deep location, and shape.

Given the imaging description "oblong structure isodense to muscular planes extending from lateral border of latissimus dorsi to pectoralis major," this strongly supports a Langer’s axillary arch anomaly rather than pathological adenopathy or tumour.
</analysis>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 23-year-old female presented with acute pain and swelling in her left ankle following a fall down the stairs. Clinical examination showed severe bilateral pes planus and mild hindfoot varus.
 Imaging Findings: Radiographic evaluation demonstrated flattening and contour irregularity of the middle and lateral thirds of the navicular bone, accompanied by sclerotic bone remodelling (Figure 1a). A step-off deformity was noted at the dorsal aspect between the navicular and cuneiform bones on the lateral view, along with flattening of the talar dome (Figure 1b).
 
 MRI confirmed these findings, revealing deformity and flattening of the central and lateral portions of the navicular bone, with bone marrow oedema predominantly in the central and dorsal regions. In addition, fragmentation of the central navicular bone was observed (Figures 2a, 2b, 3a, 3b, and 4).

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Köhler disease (childhood navicular osteochondrosis)  
   - Pros: Age can be young; navicular involvement.  
   - Cons: Köhler disease typically occurs in children (4-6 years old), not a 23-year-old; usually resolves without deformity or fragmentation; no chronic deformity or fragmentation at this age.  

2) Müller–Weiss syndrome  
   - Pros: Adult onset navicular deformity; flattening and fragmentation of navicular bone; involvement of the medial and lateral navicular with dorsal deformity; flattening of the talar dome; bone marrow edema; step-off deformity at navicular-cuneiform joint.  
   - Cons: Less common but typically middle-aged adults; trauma may exacerbate or unmask symptoms.  

3) Osteoarthritis  
   - Pros: Flattening of talar dome possible; can affect ankle joint.  
   - Cons: Primary osteoarthritis would affect joint space narrowing, osteophytes, subchondral sclerosis; navicular fragmentation and marrow edema less typical, especially in young patient; traumatic onset less likely.  

4) Navicular stress fracture  
   - Pros: Bone marrow edema and fracture possible after trauma.  
   - Cons: Would show a fracture line; fragmentation and deformity of the navicular bone less common; no explicit fracture line described; step-off deformity and chronic remodeling less typical for an acute stress fracture.  

5) Charcot neuropathic osteoarthropathy  
   - Pros: Navicular bone fragmentation and deformity possible.  
   - Cons: Usually in diabetic or neuropathic patients; no mention of neuropathy; patient is young with no underlying comorbidities; acute trauma history fits less well.

2) Köhler disease - age mismatch and chronic changes less likely.  
3) Navicular stress fracture - trauma present but no fracture line or mention; fragmentation is more chronic.  
4) Charcot neuropathy - no neuropathy history or risk factors.  
5) Osteoarthritis - less likely given age and specific navicular bone changes.  
</analysis>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 12-year-old boy without significant previous medical history presented in the ultrasound department for evaluation of a palpable lump noticed since the last two weeks in the left breast. The lump was non-tender and showed no changes in the overlying skin. There was no history of medication use. The hormonal profile and routine CBC profile were normal.
 Imaging Findings: Focused high-resolution ultrasound of both breasts and each axilla was performed using a high-frequency linear probe. The ultrasound identified a well-defined, well-encapsulated, hypoechoic lesion with smooth margins (Figure 1). It measured 3.8 x 2 x 3 cm in size and involved the left breast at the 12 o’clock periareolar region. The lesion was oriented parallel to the tissue planes (Figure 3). A single gentle lobulation was seen in this lesion. No significant vascularity was displayed on the colour Doppler study (Figure 2). The right breast was normal, and both axillae were normal.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 4-month-old male child from a tuberculosis (TB) endemic region presented with complaints of poor feeding, loss of weight for a month, and a low-grade, evening fever without any associated rash or chills for 15 days. The patient also had jerky movements of the right upper and lower limbs without any other signs of seizure disorder.
 
 The patient showed positive Kernig’s (pain and resistance to knee extension on flexing the patient’s hip and knee to 90 degrees and then attempting to extend the knee) [1] and Brudziński’s signs (in a patient with supine position, positive Brudziński’s is involuntary flexion of the hips and knees when the patient’s neck is passively flexed) [2].
 Imaging Findings: As the patient showed positive Kernig’s and Brudziński’s signs, meningeal TB was suspected. However, the chest radiograph was normal. OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An eight-month-old female child presented with jaundice and abdominal distension. Clinical examination revealed icterus, a distended stomach, and prominent superficial veins. Laboratory reports showed elevated serum glutamic pyruvic transaminase and serum glutamic oxaloacetic transaminase levels with conjugated hyperbilirubinemia.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound (USG) showed grossly coarsened echotexture of liver with multiple tiny communicating and non-communicating cystic lesions (Figures 1a and 1c). A slightly irregular liver outline was also noted (Figure 1c), indicating liver cirrhosis. Figure 1b shows a large intrahepatic cyst near the porta hepatis containing echogenic debris in dependent location, measuring 2.2 x 1.1 cm (indicating Caroli disease). Many sections also showed ill-defined hypoechoic nodules in the liver, indicative of dysplastic nodules (Figure 1d). The gallbladder was distended, showing an elongated gallbladder neck with a thickened wall (2.4 mm) and increased pericholecystic echogenicity (Figure 2a). Mild dilatation of the cystic duct was also noted (Figure 2b). Another section showed both liver and right kidney in profile with increased renal parenchymal echotexture (Figure 3). Colour Doppler assessment showed absent colour flow in cystic structures, suggesting cystic lesions to be biliary dilatations (Figures 4a and 4b). A splenomegaly of 8 cm with a dilated (10 mm) splenic vein was noted (Figure 6).

  - Grossly coarsened liver echotexture with irregular outline indicating cirrhosis.  
  - Multiple tiny communicating and non-communicating cystic lesions in the liver, with a large intrahepatic cyst near the porta hepatis containing echogenic debris (suggesting bile stasis within dilated ducts, typical of Caroli disease).  
  - Ill-defined hypoechoic nodules consistent with dysplastic nodules (common in congenital hepatic fibrosis and cirrhosis).  
  - Distended gallbladder with thickened wall and mild cystic duct dilatation (possible stasis/obstruction).  
  - Increased renal parenchymal echotexture (possible renal involvement).  
  - Splenomegaly and dilated splenic vein (portal hypertension).  
  - Colour Doppler: absent flow in cystic structures indicates they are bile duct dilatations, not vascular.

   Pros: Coarsened liver echotexture, irregular outline, dysplastic nodules, splenomegaly with portal hypertension.  
   Cons: Presence of multiple communicating cystic lesions, large intrahepatic cyst near the porta hepatis with echogenic debris, thickened gallbladder and kidney echotexture changes are not typical for viral/alcoholic cirrhosis. Also, alcoholic cirrhosis unlikely in an 8-month-old child.

   Cons: None significant; best explanation for all combined findings.

2) Caroli disease – explains bile duct cysts but not cirrhosis and portal hypertension fully.  
3) Cirrhosis (viral or alcoholic) – does not explain the multiple cystic liver lesions or renal changes; alcoholic cirrhosis impossible in child; viral cirrhosis unlikely to cause bile duct dilatations or kidney involvement.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 11-year-old girl with normal hearing and cognition had severe dysarthria and language delay since birth. Neurological exam showed no motor deficits but significant expressive impairment. Given the persistent speech delay, a brain MRI was requested for further evaluation.
 Imaging Findings: Brain MRI images demonstrated the eye-of-the-tiger sign. There is marked hypointensity of the globus pallidus on T2-weighted and FLAIR sequences secondary to iron deposition, containing minimal dot-like hyperintensities (vacuolisation), associated with comma-shaped high-signal intensity in the antero-medial part of the globus pallidus, likely due to gliosis (Figures 1 and 2). There is also marked hypointensity on both T2*EG and DWI (diffusion-weighted images) sequences due to susceptibility effects (Figures 3 and 4).
PostDescription:

There is also marked hypointensity on both T2*EG and DWI (diffusion-weighted images) sequences due to susceptibility effects.

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>
A) Key findings:
- An 11-year-old girl with severe dysarthria and expressive language delay since birth, normal hearing and cognition.
- No motor deficits, but significant expressive speech impairment.
- Brain MRI shows the classic "eye-of-the-tiger" sign in the globus pallidus: T2 and FLAIR hypointensity due to iron deposition with central hyperintense dot-like foci corresponding to gliosis and vacuolization.
- Marked susceptibility artifacts on T2* and DWI supporting iron accumulation.

B) Candidate pass/fail:
1) Wilson’s disease:
   - Pros: Basal ganglia involvement possible; may have neurological symptoms.
   - Cons: Typically involves liver dysfunction; eye-of-the-tiger sign is not characteristic; presentation usually broader with hepatic and neuropsychiatric symptoms.
2) Aceruloplasminemia:
   - Pros: Iron accumulation in brain.
   - Cons: Usually presents later in life; often with anemia, diabetes; no typical eye-of-the-tiger sign.
3) Neurodegeneration with brain iron accumulation (NBIA) disorder:
   - Pros: Eye-of-the-tiger sign is pathognomonic; iron deposition in globus pallidus; fits clinical picture.
   - Cons: NBIA includes several subtypes.
4) Pantothenate kinase-associated neurodegeneration:
   - Pros: Subtype of NBIA; classic eye-of-the-tiger sign; early onset with speech involvement possible.
   - Cons: None significant.
5) Infantile bilateral striatal necrosis:
   - Pros: Basal ganglia involvement in children.
   - Cons: Typically involves caudate and putamen acutely, not iron deposition or eye-of-the-tiger sign.
6) Organophosphate poisoning:
   - Pros: Can cause basal ganglia changes.
   - Cons: Acute toxic exposure, clinical history lacking; no iron deposition or eye-of-the-tiger sign.

C) Ranking with tie-breaks:
1) Pantothenate kinase-associated neurodegeneration (PKAN) - a subtype of NBIA, directly associated with eye-of-the-tiger sign and early-onset dysarthria and speech delay.
2) Neurodegeneration with brain iron accumulation (NBIA) disorder - umbrella term that includes PKAN.
3) Wilson’s disease, aceruloplasminemia, infantile bilateral striatal necrosis, organophosphate poisoning - less likely given clinical and imaging features.

Conclusion: The presence of the eye-of-the-tiger sign in an 11-year-old with severe dysarthria and speech delay is most characteristic of Pantothenate kinase-associated neurodegeneration (PKAN), a subtype of NBIA.

</analysis>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A nine-year-old boy, previously healthy, presented with fever, cough, dyspnoea, vomiting and diarrhoea. Eighteen days after hospital admission, he had the first spontaneous bilateral pneumothorax, needing drainage, intensive care unit admission, and mechanical ventilation. After 3 months, even with remaining lung lesions and pneumothorax, he could be discharged for outpatient follow-up.
 Imaging Findings: The initial imaging exam was a chest radiograph (X-ray) showing a bilateral diffuse micronodular acinar infiltrate (Figures 1a and 1b). A computed tomography (CT) was performed, which revealed a diffuse micronodular pattern and random distribution throughout the lung parenchyma, suggestive of an inflammatory/infectious process (Figures 2a, 2b, and 2c).
 
 The patient experienced several pneumothoraces, all of which were drained. Up to three chest tubes were placed simultaneously (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, 3d, 3e, and 3f). A second contrast-enhanced chest CT revealed confluent cystic lesions, prevailing on the upper lobes and posterior regions (Figures 4a, 4b, 4c, 4d, 4e, and 4f).
 
 A few weeks later, another contrast-enhanced chest CT showed the same findings, but with larger pneumothoraces on both sides, despite the presence of three thoracic drains (Figures 5a, 5b, 5c, 5d, and 5e). Pneumomediastinum was noted.
 
 Before hospital discharge, the patient underwent a new contrast-enhanced chest CT. The lung abnormalities persisted, but the patient was able to tolerate them even after the chest tubes were removed (Figures 6a, 6b, and 6c).
 
 Eight months later, a follow-up chest CT revealed almost complete recovery of the pulmonary abnormalities, with sequelae changes and discreet cystic lesions, and with favourable evolution of the imaging findings over the eleven-month follow-up period (Figures 7a, 7b, and 7c).

A) Key findings:  
- Age and clinical context: nine-year-old boy, previously healthy. Acute infectious presentation with fever, cough, dyspnoea, vomiting, diarrhoea.  
- Initial imaging: Chest X-ray showed bilateral diffuse micronodular acinar infiltrates.  
- CT findings: Diffuse micronodular pattern with random distribution throughout lung parenchyma, suggestive of inflammatory/infectious process.  
- Clinical course: Multiple bilateral pneumothoraces (three chest tubes placed simultaneously), pneumomediastinum, persistent lung abnormalities for months, with gradual improvement and near resolution after 11 months.  
- Imaging evolution: Development of confluent cystic lesions predominately in the upper lobes and posterior regions; later resolution with discreet cystic lesions remaining.  
- No mention of any family history, smoking, or systemic symptoms beyond the acute infection.  

B) Candidate pass/fail with 1-2 pros/cons each:  

1) Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia (PJP):  
Pros: Can cause micronodular/infiltrates, diffuse ground-glass opacities; pneumothorax can occur, especially in immunocompromised patients.  
Cons: Patient is previously healthy without known immunosuppression; PJP typically shows diffuse ground-glass rather than random micronodules; cystic changes and pneumatoceles are less common in immunocompetent children; pneumothorax is not typical but can rarely occur. However, PJP can cause spontaneous pneumothorax. The cystic change predominance in upper lobes and posterior areas is less typical.

2) Lymphoid interstitial pneumonia (LIP):  
Pros: Can cause diffuse ground glass and cystic changes; usually associated with immunodeficiency or connective tissue disease; cough and dyspnoea.  
Cons: Typically occurs in females, adolescents or young adults; often associated with underlying systemic disease; cysts are usually irregular and subpleural; no mention of underlying pathology; clinical presentation is unusual in a healthy child.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 73-year-old female patient underwent follow-up at our institution for 14 years due to a pancreatic head lesion. The lesion was monitored at six-month intervals through ultrasound, computed tomography, or magnetic resonance imaging, revealing sustained growth throughout the surveillance period. However, the patient remained asymptomatic, and laboratory tests were unremarkable.
 Imaging Findings: Cross-sectional imaging of the upper abdomen, namely computed tomography (CT) and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), revealed a multicystic lesion in the pancreatic head, with lobulated margins, enhancing internal septations (Figures 1, 2, and 3) and a central fibrotic scar with stellate calcifications (Figures 1a–1h). On ultrasound, the lesion depicts multiple hypoechoic/anechoic cysts and hyperechoic septations (Figure 4).
 
 Over time, the lesion’s enlargement led to gradual dilatation of both the bile ducts and the main pancreatic duct. In the most recent magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography (MRCP) (Figures 2a–2i), the pancreatic lesion measured approximately 97 x 86 x 126 mm (length x width x anteroposterior axis). The common bile duct had a calibre of 15 mm, and the main pancreatic duct had a maximum calibre of 7 mm. The pancreatic parenchyma was atrophic.

- Resultant mass effect causing gradual dilation of both main pancreatic duct (7 mm) and common bile duct (15 mm), with pancreatic parenchymal atrophy.  
- Imaging modalities include CT, MRI, and MRCP, the latter showing lesion size approximately 97 x 86 x 126 mm.

- Pros: Characteristic microcystic nature with enhancing septations; central scar with stellate calcifications is classic for serous cystadenoma; slow, long-standing growth aligns with benign behavior; typical in females and pancreatic head; lesion causing ductal dilatation and parenchymal atrophy consistent with large size.  
- Cons: Less commonly huge, but giant forms are reported.

2) Macrocystic serous cystadenoma of the pancreas  
- Pros: May present as a cystic lesion with enhancing septations; less classic than microcystic but documented.  
- Cons: Usually lack the central scar and stellate calcifications; central scar is more characteristic of microcystic type; macrocystic serous cystadenomas tend to have larger cysts without typical stellate pattern.

3) Mucinous cystic neoplasm of the pancreas  
- Pros: Cystic lesion in pancreatic head is possible; mucinous cystic neoplasms can have septations.  
- Cons: Typically occurs in middle-aged women but usually peripheral locations; generally lack central fibrotic scar and stellate calcifications; no mention of mural nodules or high-risk features; lesions generally do not cause ductal dilatation by mass effect as extensively; cystic neoplasm fluid content differs (mucinous).  

4) Intraductal papillary mucinous neoplasm (IPMN) of the pancreas  
- Pros: Can cause ductal dilation; cystic lesion within pancreatic duct system.  
- Cons: Usually presents dilatation of main pancreas duct with cystic communication; no mention of communication with ducts; central scar with stellate calcification not typical; IPMN tends to be less lobulated and septated; intraductal nature would be more evident on MRCP.  

5) Solid pseudopapillary neoplasm of the pancreas  
- Pros: Can present as cystic lesion; typical in young females, sometimes large.  
- Cons: Rare in this age group (73), usually solid-cystic with solid components rather than just multilobulated cystic with septations; no central scar or calcifications; less likely to have a prolonged asymptomatic course with multicystic features and septations.

3) Mucinous cystic neoplasm of the pancreas (possible but lacks hallmark central scar and ductal involvement is more typical)  
4) IPMN (ductal dilation present, but lesion morphology and features not classic)  
5) Solid pseudopapillary neoplasm (least likely given age and imaging pattern)  

</analysis>  
<final>  
Giant microcystic serous cystadenoma of the pancreas  
</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 78-year-old female presented to the surgery outpatient department with complaints of painless swelling, posterior to the left knee, for 20 years, with a recent onset of pain (since 3 months) after getting hit by an animal at the site of swelling. On examination, the swelling was firm and lobulated with no overlying skin changes. There was a history of weight loss and loss of appetite. No other comorbidities. No family history of malignancy.
 Imaging Findings: A plain lateral radiograph of the knee demonstrated a soft tissue lesion seen on the posterior aspect of the knee (Figure 1).
 
 Soft tissue ultrasound (US) demonstrated a large lobulated and irregularly shaped heterogeneously hypoechoic mass lesion with internal vascularity, seen posterior to the left knee (Figure 2 ). A few central hypoechoic necrotic/cystic areas were also seen. The mass was also seen displacing the popliteal vessels superficially.
 
 MRI (with contrast) findings revealed a large T1 heterointense lobulated mass lesion in the popliteal fossa with patchy areas of T1 hyperintensity (Figure 3). It also showed heterointense signal on T2W images (Figure 4). The lesion also showed patchy areas of diffusion restriction on DWI and ADC (Figures 5a and 5b) and heterogeneous enhancement on post-contrast study (Figure 6). It was seen displacing and causing near-complete encasement of the popliteal neurovascular bundle. There was no obvious involvement/infiltration of the overlying skin.

A) Key findings:
- Elderly female (78 years old) with long-standing painless lobulated swelling posterior to left knee, recent pain after trauma.
- Imaging: Soft tissue mass in popliteal fossa, heterogeneously hypoechoic on US with internal vascularity and central necrotic/cystic areas.
- MRI: Large lobulated mass in popliteal fossa, T1 heterointense with patchy T1 hyperintensity (suggestive of melanin or hemorrhage), T2 heterointense, diffusion restriction, heterogeneous enhancement.
- Mass displaces and encases popliteal neurovascular bundle without skin infiltration.
- No skin changes, but history of systemic symptoms (weight loss, appetite loss) raising concern for malignancy.
- Chronicity of lesion (20 years) with recent change and pain after trauma.
- Presence of melanin-like signal on MRI (patchy T1 hyperintensity).

B) Candidate pass/fail with pros/cons:

1) Clear cell sarcoma
   - Pros: Usually affects young adults, commonly in extremities near aponeuroses, can have melanin-like T1 hyperintensity.
   - Cons: Rare in elderly females, typically not in popliteal fossa; more common in tendons/aponeuroses; history does not fit classic demographics.

2) Malignant melanoma
   - Pros: Melanoma can arise in soft tissue, shows T1 hyperintensity due to melanin; accounts for nodular lesions with internal necrosis and enhancement.
   - Cons: No skin changes seen; no known primary lesion; primary extra-cutaneous soft tissue melanoma is rare; weight loss could suggest metastasis, but less likely to be primary in popliteal fossa.

3) Clear cell soft tissue sarcoma with melanocytic differentiation
   - Pros: Clear cell sarcoma is also called malignant melanoma of soft tissue; shows T1 hyperintensity due to melanin; commonly in young adults but can occur in elderly.
   - Cons: Typically occurs in distal extremities; popliteal fossa location less common but possible.

4) Pigment villonodular synovitis with malignant transformation
   - Pros: Pigment villonodular synovitis (PVNS) occurs in joints; melanin-like T1 hyperintensity possible due to hemosiderin or pigment; rare malignant transformation could cause necrosis and enhancement.
   - Cons: PVNS usually involves joint synovium, not large lobulated soft tissue mass displacing neurovascular bundle; lesion chronic but PVNS malignant transformation is extremely rare and usually intra-articular.

5) Synovial sarcoma
   - Pros: Common in young adults around joints; lobulated mass near knee; heterogeneous enhancement; can show hemorrhage/necrosis.
   - Cons: Usually younger patients; no melanin-like T1 hyperintensity typical; no mention of calcifications.

6) Alveolar soft part sarcoma
   - Pros: Rare; can be in extremities with slow growth; heterogeneous enhancement.
   - Cons: Usually young adults; no melanin-like signal; more common in young females or males.

7) Malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumour (MPNST)
   - Pros: Can occur near nerves (popliteal neurovascular bundle); large lobulated mass with necrosis and enhancement; common in older patients; history of nerve involvement.
   - Cons: Melanin-like T1 hyperintensity not typical; no skin changes; no known neurofibromatosis.

C) Ranking:

1) Clear cell soft tissue sarcoma with melanocytic differentiation (aka clear cell sarcoma)
   - Best fit for T1 melanin-like hyperintensity, lobulated mass, location near extremity, internal necrosis, and neurovascular encasement. Although demographics slightly off, the melanocytic differentiation is a hallmark, and it resembles melanoma but is classified as soft tissue sarcoma.
2) Malignant melanoma
   - Considered due to melanin signal and soft tissue mass; however, lack of skin primary, chronicity, and location less likely.
3) Synovial sarcoma
   - Possible, but melanin-like T1 hyperintensity not typical, and patient age less typical.
4) Malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumour
   - Fits location and enhancement but lacks melanin features.
5) Pigment villonodular synovitis with malignant transformation
   - Least likely due to lesion size, location, and rarity of malignant transformation.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 36-year-old male, HIV-1 positive since age 22 with no follow-up for 4 years, presented with progressive dyspnoea, cough, thoracic pain, and fatigue. Initially treated for presumed community-acquired pneumonia with partial symptom improvement, but later experienced worsening respiratory symptoms. Denied fever or night sweats.
 Imaging Findings: Chest X-ray revealed mediastinal widening, likely due to a mass, accompanied by ill-defined lower lung opacities and bilateral pleural effusion, more pronounced on the right (Figure 1).
 
 Thoracic CT demonstrated a large anterior mediastinal mass (10.8 × 5.3 × 7.6 cm), heterogeneously enhancing with cystic areas and displacement of adjacent vascular structures without invasion, likely representing a conglomerate of necrotic hypervascular lymph nodes (Figure 2). Additional mediastinal adenopathies were noted.
 
 Pulmonary findings included bilateral “flame-shaped” peribronchovascular opacities (Figure 3a), patchy ground-glass opacities, and interlobular septal thickening (Figure 3b). Bilateral pleural effusion was also evident. Systemic hypervascular adenopathies involved the axillary, mesenteric, and inguinal regions bilaterally (short diameter up to 1.7 cm) (Figures 4a, 4b, and 4c).
 
 Abdominal imaging revealed cutaneous millimetric nodules in the thoracic and abdominal walls (Figure 5), hepatomegaly (17.9 cm in craniocaudal length), and gastric wall thickening (Figures 6a and 6b).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 19-day-old female newborn was brought to our hospital because of left breast tumefaction for 2 days, with localised erythema and irritability. No fluctuance or nipple discharge was noted. No fever was present. The white blood cell count was elevated. The delivery was eutocic (40 weeks and 2 days) and unremarkable.
 Imaging Findings: The right breast was unremarkable (Figure 1a). The breast bud is hypoechoic relative to subcutaneous fat. OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 30‐year‐old man presented with two months of right knee pain and swelling. He had recovered from COVID‑19 pneumonia three months earlier after receiving systemic steroids. During his stay in the hospital, the patient has received dexamethasone injection 8 mg/day through the intravenous route for 10 days with gradual tapering over the next 2 weeks. No evidence of diabetes mellitus or other systemic comorbidities. Localised tenderness over the patella was noted; there were no systemic symptoms or history of trauma.
 Imaging Findings: Radiographs of the knee in anteroposterior, lateral, and skyline views revealed superficial sclerosis of the patella, along with a subtle erosion at its inferior pole (Figures 1a and 1b). There were no signs of fracture or significant soft tissue injury. Given the clinical context and radiographic findings, infection was suspected, and an MRI was recommended.
 
 On MRI, the patellar inferior pole appeared hypointense on T1 images (Figures 2a and 2b) and hyperintense on proton density (PD) fat-saturated sequences (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c). Diffusion-weighted images (DWI) also indicated restriction (Figures 4a and 4b). The subchondral bone and articular cartilage remained largely intact, although a mild effusion was present. Post-contrast images showed thickening and enhancement of the synovium (Figures 5a and 5b). Osteomyelitis of the patella was considered likely.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 45-year-old female presented with a longstanding history of primary amenorrhea and cyclical lower abdominal pain since adolescence. The pain occurs every month, typically lasting 3–5 days, and is described as crampy and localised to the lower abdomen and pelvis.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound examination done on a Samsung RS80A ultrasound machine showed a bulky uterine body with hypoechoic endometrial collection (Figure 1). A heterogeneously hypoechoic lesion with internal echogenic debris was noted in the right adnexa, arising from the right ovary (Figure 2). The left ovary appears normal.
 
 Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) was performed using a 1.5 Tesla GE MR machine. T2-weighted sagittal and axial images of the MRI pelvis show the presence of the fundus and body of the uterus, which is oriented in the right lateral position in the pelvic cavity, with the absence of the cervix (Figure 3). However, the vagina is visualised. T1 hyperintense and T2 mildly hypointense endometrial collection is noted within the endometrial cavity (Figures 3 and 4). A well-defined T1 hyperintense and predominantly T2 hypointense (T2 shading sign) cystic lesion with a thin wall is noted in the right adnexa, arising from the right ovary (Figures 5 and 6). Minimal pelvic ascites is noted.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 54-year-old man known to have hypertension and type 2 diabetes experienced paraesthesia in his left hand and distal part of his left forearm for several months. On examination, multiple well-defined skin nodules were found, globally distributed, along with reduced sensation distally in the left upper limb.
 Imaging Findings: MRI of the brachial plexus with intravenous contrast showed multiple well-marginated lesions with heterogeneous signal in the infraclavicular part of the left brachial plexus, including the trunks and divisions (Figures 1, 2a, 2b, and 3), extending into the axilla and proximal parts of the upper arm, suggestive of neurofibromas.
 
 MRI of the spine and brain showed no abnormalities, including vestibular schwannoma, and the patient was diagnosed with schwannomatosis.

MRI of the spine and brain showed no abnormalities, including vestibular schwannoma, and the patient was diagnosed with schwannomatosis.
